PhD Progress book Ch. 1 About 1.1 Usage 1.2 Research topic

PhD Progress book Andrew Habrich 2023-12-11 Ch. 1 About This is a book document meant to track progress on my PhD projects in a reproducible and organized report. In this document you’ll find tables, figures, and descriptive code for state of progress for each of the projects. Most of the work for these projects is done with R, and the reports (including this one) are generated with R Markdown. The functional code and folder directories for each of the R projects can be found on my github page: https://github.com/drewhabrich. Note though, that not all the README.md files are updated yet (work-in-progress). 1.1 Usage The directory on the left is clickable and should help with navigation. In addition, the whole document can be downloaded as EPUB by clicking the download button at the top of the page, if that’s your preference. 1.2 Research topic Landscape connectivity and bird and butterfly diversity in the urban ecosystems: Conceptually simple, landscape connectivity is described as the degree to which the landscape configuration affects movement of organisms (Tischendorf and Fahrig 2000; Taylor et al. 1993). Connectivity has become central to many biodiversity conservation efforts (Rudnick et al. 2012; Keeley, Beier, and Jenness 2021), including city-specific planning (Nilon et al. 2017), as well as a topic of interest in international agreements (Convention on Biological Diversity 2022). Despite the simplicity of the concept, in practice, landscape connectivity is measured using a broad range of statistical and theoretical approaches, all of which have different assumptions, interpretations and implications for landscape management (Correa Ayram et al. 2016; Fletcher et al. 2019; Fahrig et al. 2021). Recent narrative reviews on the impacts of landscape connectivity on urban biodiversity have identified inconsistencies in the literature, particularly around the clarity of statements describing a priori hyptheses, and definitions of ‘connectivity’ and ‘urban’ (LaPoint et al. 2015; Lookingbill et al. 2022). It follows that inconsistent or unclear use of connectivity concepts throughout the literature may constrain comparability between studies and complicate interpretation, limiting our capability to identify the real impact of connectivity on biodiversity As such, there remains a need to improve understanding of effects urban landscape connectivity, and a necessity to validate these connectivity models with consistent biological data across multiple cities. The projects in my thesis aim to provide clarity on this topic, and to develop transparent, reproducible frameworks to incorporate available biodiversity data and remote-sensing imagery to validate and facilitate landscape connectivity modelling in urban areas. References
Ch. 2 Meta-analysis Missed connections in the city: A meta-analysis of landscape connectivity factors affecting biodiversity in urban ecosystems

Ch. 2 Meta-analysis Missed connections in the city: A meta-analysis of landscape connectivity factors affecting biodiversity in urban ecosystems With the ongoing and expanding use of connectivity metrics in conservation planning, there is considerable value in disentangling the true effect of connectivity on biodiversity from methodological and statistical artifacts to inform sustainable urban development. I intend to review the urban connectivity literature to answer the following research questions: What is the overall direction and magnitude of its effect on urban biodiversity? What variation/heterogeneity among studies can be explained by biological factors (taxonomy, life history traits etc.) and anthropogenic factors (geographic location, city size, human population density, etc.) To what extent have urban connectivity models been validated with independently obtained biological data in cities? How does the theoretical approach/connectivity metric used depend on the taxa studied or spatial scale, and does it affect the overall direction/magnitude of the effect?

Ch. 3 ebird checklist diversity in Canadian cities The following R packages (and their versions) were used to generate this report. ## leaflet mapview patchwork vegan lattice permute ## "2.2.1" "2.11.2" "1.1.3" "2.6-4" "0.21-9" "0.9-7" ## ggpubr DataExplorer lubridate forcats stringr dplyr ## "0.6.0" "0.8.2" "1.9.3" "1.0.0" "1.5.0" "1.1.3" ## purrr readr tidyr tibble ggplot2 tidyverse ## "1.0.2" "2.1.4" "1.3.0" "3.2.1" "3.4.4" "2.0.0" ## tarchetypes targets ## "0.7.9" "1.3.2" 3.1 Research question Does the built environment lower landscape connectivity for birds in the city, limiting dispersal and species richness in patches across the city? If so, what urban features most strongly contribute to this effect on species richness? I plan to answer this research question using the ebird dataset and remote-sensed data to compare models of landscape connectivity for the most populated urban areas in Canada. Here you’ll find an overview of the framework I’ve developed to answer my research questions. 3.2 ebird dataset download First, the dataset needs to be downloaded and cleaned up to get the relevant data to estimate species diversity. Note however, that the complete dataset as of Aug. 2023 is >500 gb of text (impossible to open with a text editor). Access requires use of a custom R package, auk(https://cornelllabofornithology.github.io/auk/). The dataset itself is downloadable with a free account on the ebird website (https://ebird.org/home) or through Global biodiversity information facility (https://www.gbif.org/dataset/4fa7b334-ce0d-4e88-aaae-2e0c138d049e) eBird and other community-collected datasets are generally “semi-structured” checklists where individuals can report both incidental observations, and complete surveys. To estimate biodiversity, the data needs to be filtered to minimize biases in the metrics of interest (species richness, eveness, etc.). After initial data tidying and filtering, the dataset can be filtered using including geographic shapefiles to extract research-quality data by region of interest, i.e. Canadian urban areas. 3.3 Methods Due to the enormity of the complete dataset, I needed a systematic way of filtering the dataset that was semi-automated, transparent, repeatable and not reliant on computationally intense for-loops. Using the targets R package (https://docs.ropensci.org/targets/), an open-source pipeline tool, and a collection of custom functions I’ve written, I developed the following pipeline to: Filter the original ebird dataset to the latest Canadian census data urban area delination for cities with >100000 people (2021, https://www12.statcan.gc.ca/census-recensement/2021/dp-pd/index-eng.cfm), buffered by 5km to capture any data just outside the city limits. Format and tidy datasets to calculate biodiversity estimates for complete checklists. Calculate some biodiversity metrics (Right now only species richness, shannon diversity, evenness; other estimates as well as city-wide estimates can be added to the pipeline) Targets workflow network. Circles are R objects, and squares are objects that are iterated over for each subset of datapoints for the each city. Each of these steps can be updated, changed, or branched from, and the whole pipeline rerun, with only the affected downstream objects needing reanalysis. This framework makes it easy to repeat the analysis for each city that is included without repeating the time-consuming steps for each city independently. Below I provide an overview what the dataset looks like for only one city. 3.4 Ottawa - Gatineau Here we can see the number of unique, complete checklists for Ottawa - Gatineau by year. As you can see, the pandemic was a huge boon for urban birders, and the number of checklists in the city nearly doubled! Figure 3.1: Number of complete eBird checklists in Ottawa - Gatineau from 2010 to 2022 After some dataset tidying, I calculated biodiversity estimates for each of the checklists. Note this is super rough, and does not take into consideration checklist sampling effort or locations across the city. Below is a series of violin plots, showing the distribution of the diversity metric across checklists for a given year. Figure 3.2: Species richness estimates from eBird checklists for Ottawa - Gatineau for each year from 2010 - 2022 Figure 3.3: Species evenness estimates from eBird checklists for Ottawa - Gatineau for each year from 2010 - 2022 Figure 3.4: Shannon diversity estimates from eBird checklists for Ottawa - Gatineau for each year from 2010 - 2022 Here you can see a summary (n# of checklists, median value, inter-quartile range) of the estimated biodiversity metrics for each year of complete checklists in the Ottawa - Gatineau urban area. Looks pretty consistent across years, which suggests that there’s no immediately obvious source of bias in the dataset. We can also take a look at the effort data for the checklists, to provides more information on the potential biases could come from different sampling methods. Figure 3.5: Overview of sampling effort for checklists 3.5 Interactive map Species richness estimates for complete eBird checklists in Ottawa - Gatineau for 2010 (left) and 2020 (right). 3.6 Next steps: Check checklist level habitat and land cover variables in area around each points. Select points and control for habitat amount, so that when I get to the modelling we can account for the effects of habitat amount on the relationship between the variables. Write scripts to incorporate landscapemetrics package into workflow. Estimate structural connectivity from landscape metrics (distance between checklists). Develope connectivity modelling that incorporate species informations. Be a cool scientist and write up stuff. Have a career as a cool scientist???

Ch. 4 ebfly data in canadian cities The following R packages (and their versions) were used to generate this report. ## ggspatial geodata sf terra leafsync leaflet ## "1.1.9" "0.5-9" "1.0-14" "1.7-55" "0.1.0" "2.2.1" ## mapview patchwork vegan lattice permute ggpubr ## "2.11.2" "1.1.3" "2.6-4" "0.21-9" "0.9-7" "0.6.0" ## DataExplorer lubridate forcats stringr dplyr purrr ## "0.8.2" "1.9.3" "1.0.0" "1.5.0" "1.1.3" "1.0.2" ## readr tidyr tibble ggplot2 tidyverse tarchetypes ## "2.1.4" "1.3.0" "3.2.1" "3.4.4" "2.0.0" "0.7.9" ## targets ## "1.3.2" 4.1 ebutterfly data download The most recent version of the dataset was downloaded from the Global Biodiversity Information Facility (https://www.gbif.org/dataset/cf3bdc30-370c-48d3-8fff-b587a39d72d6) . Note that downloading the data using the scripts on the github page (https://github.com/drewhabrich/Urban_eBfly) requires an account on the gbif website. After removing incidental and museum records, checking for complete checklists by province/state shows an uneven distribution checklists in the dataset. Figure 4.1: Number of complete eButterfly checklists by State/Province 4.2 Checklists filtered by geography Using the latest Canadian census data (2021, https://www12.statcan.gc.ca/census-recensement/2021/dp-pd/index-eng.cfm) filtered by urban area delination with >50000 people, buffered by 5km to capture any data just outside the city limits. Figure 4.2: # of complete eButterfly checklists in the most populated Canadian cities coloured by Province. The vertical black line is at 100 checklists, which may be the lower limit to get accurate estimates of species diversity (I need to investigate species accumulation curves to see the comprehensiveness of the surveys). As you can see, the distribution of effort is not uniformly distributed across cities in Canada. 4.3 Checklists by year of submission It is also important to consider the years that the checklists were submitted, in order to find remote-sensing imagery that matches temporal and spatial scale for landscape connectivity modelling. Here we can see the number of checklists by province as well. Figure 4.3: Number of complete eButterfly checklists by year of submission, coloured by Province Fairly uneven distribution across years, so maybe its better to limit analysis to years with sufficient data? 4.4 Ottawa - Gatineau Let’s take a closer look at the single city with the most number of checklists: Figure 4.4: Checklist density across the city of Ottawa - Gatineau. Lighter colours indicate a higher density. Note the second figure with the # of checklists by year ## | | | 0% | |======== | 11% | |================ | 22% | |======================= | 33% | |=============================== | 44% | |======================================= | 56% | |=============================================== | 67% | |====================================================== | 78% | |============================================================== | 89% | |======================================================================| 100% Figure 4.5: Checklist density across the city of Ottawa - Gatineau. Lighter colours indicate a higher density. Note the second figure with the # of checklists by year 4.5 Interactive map You can interactively browse the dataset here, I’ve provided 2 layers to visualize the distribution of checklists by 1) sampling protocol, 2) year of checklist submission. You can turn them on and off with the layers button on the left (the square button with the image of stacked squares). Clicking on one of the points will display the attributes for the checklist. Figure 4.6: Interactive map of eButterfly checklists in Ottawa - Gatineau 4.6 Next steps: Check to see if there is ebutterfly data for cities in the US (probably just Vermont or a few states) Calculate biodiversity estimates Estimate structural connectivity from landscape metrics (distance between checklists) Connectivity modelling ???
